findings.summary: The common/ package contains 13 modules spanning concerns from config and logging to tag lifecycle, claims, document iteration, and LLM wiring. While each module is individually focused, the package itself has no internal decomposition, making it a catch-all for anything shared. A new engineer cannot predict where a given concern lives without scanning all files.
evidence: 13 modules in src/common/ with no sub-packages
tags.py, claims.py, stale_lock.py, and document_iter.py all deal with the tag-based processing lifecycle but sit alongside unrelated modules like llm.py and logging_config.py
bootstrap.py imports 7 sibling modules from common/ — a sign the package has become a hub
findings.suggestion: Consider grouping tag-lifecycle modules (tags.py, claims.py, stale_lock.py, document_iter.py) into a common/pipeline sub-package to give navigational structure without changing APIs.

findings.summary: ocr/daemon.py and classifier/daemon.py follow identical structural patterns (bootstrap, log startup params, define process_document closure, call run_polling_threadpool, close clients in finally) but with no shared abstraction. The two daemons differ only in which provider/processor they instantiate and which tag IDs they use.
evidence: Both daemon.py files: bootstrap_daemon() -> log.info('Starting...') -> try: run_polling_threadpool(...) finally: client.close()
Both define a process_document function that creates a PaperlessClient, a provider, a processor, calls processor.process(), and closes the client in finally
classifier/daemon.py adds only taxonomy_cache and before_each_batch as differences
findings.suggestion: A small shared daemon runner function or base class that accepts a factory for the processor/provider could eliminate the structural duplication while keeping domain-specific wiring in each package.

findings.summary: OcrProvider.transcribe_image resets ThreadSafeStats at the start of every call (self._stats.reset(self._STAT_KEYS)). Since the provider is instantiated per-document and transcribe_image is called per-page, this means stats from page 1 are wiped before page 2 runs. The final stats snapshot returned by get_stats() only reflects the last page processed, not the full document.
evidence: provider.py line 65: self._stats.reset(self._STAT_KEYS) — called at the start of transcribe_image
worker.py calls transcribe_image once per page in _ocr_pages_in_parallel, then calls get_stats() once at the end
Only the last page's stats survive, so aggregate doc-level stats (total attempts, total refusals) are lost
findings.suggestion: Move the stats reset to the document level (e.g. in worker.py before the page loop starts) or remove the per-call reset entirely since the provider is already per-document.

findings.summary: PaperlessClient._create_named_item raises AssertionError (typo for AssertionError) on an unreachable path. While it is marked pragma: no cover, the typo suggests the error was never exercised even manually.
evidence: paperless.py line 117: raise AssertionError(... — should be AssertionError; Python will raise NameError at runtime if this path is ever reached
findings.suggestion: Fix the typo to AssertionError or replace with a plain RuntimeError/AssertionError.

findings.summary: _check_llm_reachable() in preflight.py uses the module-level openai.models.list() which relies on the module-level default openai client, not the explicitly configured client stored in _OpenAIClientHolder. This means the preflight check may succeed/fail based on environment variables rather than the actual configured client the daemons will use.
evidence: preflight.py line 73: openai.models.list() — uses the default module-level openai client
library_setup.py creates a custom OpenAI client with explicit http_client and base_url, stored via init_openai_client()
The preflight check bypasses this configured client entirely
findings.suggestion: Use the holder's client (_openai_holder.get().models.list()) so the preflight check exercises the same client the daemons will use for real work.

findings.summary: Tags are repeatedly converted between set[int] and list[int] at module boundaries. Functions like extract_tags return set[int], but the Paperless API requires list[int], so callers scatter list(...) conversions. This creates visual noise and subtle ordering concerns (sets are unordered).
evidence: tags.py: extract_tags returns set[int]; remove_stale_queue_tag calls client.update_document_metadata(tags=list(updated))
claims.py line 53: client.update_document_metadata(doc_id, tags=list(updated_tags))
worker.py (ocr) line 202: client.update_document(self.doc_id, full_text, list(current_tags))
worker.py (classifier) line 299: tags=list(current_tags)
At least 8 occurrences of list(...) wrapping a set of tags across the codebase
findings.suggestion: Consider having the update methods accept sets directly and do the conversion once internally, or use sorted(tags) for deterministic ordering in tests and logs.

findings.summary: The retry decorator's wrapper function has an implicit None return after the for loop exhausts without raising. If all retries are consumed and the exception is raised on the last attempt (line 46), this is correct. But if the retryable exception check somehow doesn't match on the last iteration, the function falls through and returns None with no type error.
evidence: retry.py lines 32-56: wrapper function has no explicit return after the for loop. If the loop completes without returning (e.g., MAX_RETRIES somehow yields 0 iterations despite the >= 1 check), the return type annotation Callable[..., T] is violated.
findings.suggestion: Add a defensive raise or return after the for loop to make the control flow exhaustive and satisfy type checkers.

findings.summary: ClassificationProvider._try_strip_compat_param dispatches error detection via getattr(self, detector_name)(error), using string-based method names stored in the _COMPAT_PARAMS tuple. This indirection makes it harder to trace which detector runs for which parameter and defeats IDE navigation.
evidence: provider.py line 62-66: _COMPAT_PARAMS stores method names as strings: ('temperature', '_is_temperature_error', 'temperature_retries')
provider.py line 101: getattr(self, detector_name)(error) — dynamic dispatch by string name
findings.suggestion: Store direct callable references (e.g., (self._is_temperature_error,)) in _COMPAT_PARAMS or use a list of dataclass/NamedTuple entries with callable fields to preserve traceability.

findings.summary: OCR refusal detection phrases are defined in two separate places with overlapping but not identical content: Settings._load_llm_settings() (OCR_REFUSAL_MARKERS default list) and classifier/constants.py (ERROR_PHRASES tuple). Both are used for the same purpose — detecting model refusals — but neither references the other.
evidence: config.py lines 93-99: default_ocr_refusal_markers includes 'i can't assist', 'i cannot assist', 'chatgpt refused to transcribe', etc.
constants.py lines 47-53: ERROR_PHRASES includes 'i'm sorry, i can't assist with that', 'chatgpt refused to transcribe', 'i can't help with transcrib', etc.
Both are passed to the same is_error_content() function in common/utils.py
findings.suggestion: Define refusal phrases once in a shared location (e.g., common/constants.py) and import from both the config defaults and classifier quality gates.

findings.summary: Both DocumentProcessor.process() (OCR) and ClassificationProcessor.process() have deep nesting (try/finally wrapping try/except wrapping if/return chains) with multiple early-return paths inside a single method. The OCR worker's process() is 75 lines with 4 levels of nesting at the deepest point.
evidence: ocr/worker.py process(): try -> if error_tag -> return, claim -> if not claimed -> return, download -> try convert -> except -> return, if not images -> return, try ocr -> finally close images, assemble, update
classifier/worker.py process(): try -> if error_tag -> return, claim -> if not claimed -> return, if not content -> return, truncate, classify, if empty -> return, if generic -> return, apply
findings.suggestion: Extract the inner phases (download-convert, ocr-pages, assemble-update) into separate methods that return early results, reducing the try/finally nesting depth in the main process() orchestrator.
